failure_modes.text: Persistence can turn into sunk-cost escalation when evidence says the effort should pivot or stop.
failure_modes.mitigation: Install kill criteria before starting: define in advance what evidence would trigger a pivot or stop, so escalation of commitment cannot disguise itself as grit.

failure_modes.text: Teams can persist in the wrong frame because the original hypothesis was never pressure-tested well enough.
failure_modes.mitigation: Periodically re-validate the hypothesis: pause persistence at defined checkpoints to ask whether the original premise still holds or whether the team is grinding on a dead frame.

failure_modes.text: Blind endurance can ignore cognitive limits and turn hard effort into poor learning or burnout.
failure_modes.mitigation: Monitor cognitive and physical load: set explicit recovery and reflection intervals so endurance does not degrade learning quality or push people into burnout.

premortem_questions.text: What evidence would tell us this is disciplined persistence versus mere doubling down?
premortem_questions.source_quote: what kind of progress would count as proof

premortem_questions.text: Is the worst case bad enough if we keep pushing the current plan?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Is the worst case bad enough?

premortem_questions.text: What would we have to believe for this continued effort to still make sense?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

heuristics.text: Use SMART goals so persistence has a clear target instead of an abstract aspiration.
Keep attention on process quality during execution and assess outcomes after the task is done.
Use interrogative self-talk to pull up specific execution strategies under pressure.
heuristics.source_quote: Can I make a great pitch?
